title: Computer Vision Fundamentals
description: Explore how computer vision technologies can support social work practice, from organizing visual information to understanding ethical considerations.

nodes.title: Introduction to Computer Vision in Social Work
nodes.content: Computer vision (CV) is a field of artificial intelligence that enables computers to interpret and analyze visual information from images and videos. In social work, CV tools can support your practice by helping you document client conditions, identify risks and resources in home environments, and organize visual information more efficiently. However, these powerful tools also come with important ethical considerations that you must understand before applying them. This course explores three key computer vision applications relevant to social work practice: classification, detection, and recognition technologies.

nodes.title: Which Computer Vision Application Interests You Most?
nodes.content: Choose the order in which you'd like to explore three important computer vision applications used in social work. You can explore examples and applications relevant to your area of practice.

choices.label: Image Classification - Organizing and categorizing visual information
Object Detection - Identifying specific items, hazards, and resources
Facial Recognition - Understanding bias and ethical implications

nodes.title: Image Classification: Organizing Visual Information
nodes.content: Image classification is a computer vision task where algorithms analyze an image and assign it to one of several predefined categories. In social work, classification can help you organize and prioritize information from case documentation photos. For example, you might use classification to quickly categorize images of client living conditions as 'safe,' 'needs improvement,' or 'requires immediate intervention.' Classification works by identifying patterns of pixels and features in images that the system has learned to recognize. This is particularly useful in managing large volumes of home assessment photos, helping ensure consistent documentation across your caseload, and flagging cases that may need immediate attention.

nodes.title: Classifying Home Safety Conditions - A Worked Example
nodes.content: Consider Maria, a case manager conducting home assessments for five new clients. She photographs each home's main living area. Using image classification, the system analyzes each photo and assigns it to one of three categories based on patterns it has learned: 'Safe environment' (adequate lighting, clear walkways, functional furniture), 'Needs improvement' (clutter, poor lighting, minor hazards), or 'High risk' (severe clutter, blocked exits, visible hazards). Maria's classification system identifies that three homes are safe, one needs improvement, and one is high-risk. This automated first pass lets Maria prioritize her limited time on the high-risk case immediately, while the 'needs improvement' home gets scheduled for targeted interventions. The system doesn't replace Maria's professional judgment—she still reviews each classification and makes final decisions about intervention levels.

nodes.title: Object Detection: Finding Specific Items and Risks
nodes.content: Object detection is a computer vision technique that identifies and locates multiple specific objects within an image, marking them with boundaries and labels. While classification answers 'what category is this overall image,' detection answers 'what specific things are in this image and where are they.' In social work, object detection can help you identify safety equipment, hazards, and resources during home visits. For instance, a detection system might locate fire extinguishers, grab bars, stair railings, exposed electrical hazards, or wheelchair accessibility features in a photo of a client's home. This technology is particularly valuable for conducting thorough safety assessments, ensuring consistent evaluation across multiple homes, and supporting documentation for intervention plans.

nodes.title: Detecting Safety Equipment and Hazards - A Worked Example
nodes.content: James is a social worker supporting elderly clients aging in place. During a home safety assessment for Mr. Chen, James photographs his bathroom. Using object detection, the system identifies and marks: a grab bar next to the toilet (present—good), a non-slip mat (present—good), exposed electrical outlet near sink (hazard—flagged), and absence of a shower chair (needed—noted). The system generates a checklist showing what safety features are present, what's missing, and what hazards exist. James uses this to create a targeted action plan: install a shower chair and cover the electrical outlet. He documents the detection results in Mr. Chen's file for follow-up visits. The same system helps James quickly scan ten more clients' bathrooms and identify that three others also lack non-slip mats, allowing him to coordinate a bulk purchase. Again, James makes all clinical decisions, but the technology helps him be more thorough and consistent.

nodes.title: Facial Recognition: Understanding Bias and Ethical Concerns
nodes.content: Facial recognition technology uses computer vision to identify or verify individuals by analyzing facial features in images or videos. While this technology has potential applications (reuniting separated families, identifying missing persons, documenting consent), it raises profound ethical concerns that social workers must understand. Research consistently shows that facial recognition systems perform differently across racial groups, ages, and genders—often with significantly higher error rates for people of color, women, and older adults. In social work contexts, these biases could lead to misidentification, privacy violations, and discriminatory outcomes. Before using any facial recognition technology, you must understand its limitations, test it for bias in your specific populations, obtain informed consent, have clear policies protecting data, and always maintain human oversight. The stakes in social work—child welfare, justice involvement, housing access—are too high to rely on biased systems.

nodes.title: Recognizing Bias in Facial Recognition Systems
nodes.content: A child protective services agency implements facial recognition to help identify individuals with prior substantiated abuse allegations when they appear in community or court photos. During testing with their actual case files, they discover the system correctly identifies individuals in 98% of cases—but only 88% for individuals of color and 82% for individuals over 65. This disparity could lead to missed matches for people of color (failing to identify risks) or false matches for older adults (wrongly flagging them). The agency discovers through further testing that the system was trained primarily on images of younger white faces. Beyond accuracy issues, the agency also recognizes that using facial recognition without explicit consent violates client trust and confidentiality norms. They decide against implementation. This example illustrates why you must: 1) Test systems with your actual population, 2) Understand training data limitations, 3) Consider consent and privacy thoroughly, and 4) Always question whether a new technology truly serves your clients' interests.

nodes.title: Integrating Computer Vision Ethically in Social Work Practice
nodes.content: Each computer vision application—classification, detection, and recognition—offers valuable potential to support your work, but all require ethical integration into social work practice. Regardless of which technology you consider, follow these principles: First, maintain human judgment and responsibility—technology should enhance your assessment, never replace it. Second, prioritize informed consent from clients; they should understand what's being documented and how. Third, protect data security; visual records contain sensitive information and must be stored safely. Fourth, test for bias in your specific populations and contexts. Fifth, be transparent with clients and supervisors about what technology does and doesn't do. Sixth, keep clear policies about data retention and access. Finally, remember that social work's primary commitment is to clients' wellbeing and self-determination. Technology is a tool; your values and judgment are irreplaceable.

nodes.title: Applying Computer Vision Ethically in Social Work
nodes.content: Apply your learning to realistic scenarios. Consider when each technology might help your practice and when ethical concerns outweigh benefits.

questions.question: You work with families experiencing homelessness. You're considering using image classification to quickly assess shelter conditions across multiple locations. What should you prioritize before implementation?
options: A) Implementing it quickly to serve more families
B) Obtaining informed consent from residents and testing accuracy with your specific population, ensuring classification supports rather than replaces your professional assessment
C) Using the technology without informing residents since it helps your documentation
D) Assuming the technology works equally well for all populations
feedback: Correct! Informed consent, testing for accuracy, and maintaining professional judgment are essential.
Transparency and consent are foundational to social work ethics.
Bias testing is critical; systems don't work equally across populations.
questions.explanation: Before using any technology with clients, you must obtain their informed consent, understand how it performs with your specific population, and ensure it genuinely supports your professional judgment rather than replacing it.

questions.question: Object detection identifies safety hazards in elderly clients' homes. A system flags missing grab bars with 95% accuracy overall, but only 78% accuracy for homes of recent immigrants. What's your professional obligation?
options: A) Use it only for majority populations where accuracy is higher
B) Don't use the technology; install grab bars in all homes regardless
C) Use the technology but be especially thorough in your manual assessment of homes in communities where accuracy is lower, and consider this a supplementary tool only
D) Use it the same way for everyone; inconsistency creates problems
feedback: Unnecessary and doesn't address the technology's actual capabilities.
Correct! Awareness of system limitations, human oversight, and adaptive practice serve clients better.
Consistency doesn't mean ignoring known system bias; it means applying ethical standards consistently.
questions.explanation: When you're aware a tool works differently for different populations, you adapt your practice accordingly—maintaining professional oversight where the tool is less reliable ensures equitable and safe service.

questions.question: A colleague suggests using facial recognition to identify individuals in youth program photos to automatically tag and organize them. What should you address first?
options: A) Whether the technology has high accuracy ratings from the vendor
B) Whether youth and families have given informed consent, understanding specifically how their images will be used and stored
D) Whether the technology is trendy and modern
feedback: Vendor claims don't ensure ethical use or unbiased performance.
Correct! Consent is foundational; you must be transparent about facial recognition's purposes, limitations, and data handling.
Approval matters, but consent from affected clients is the baseline.
Modernity is irrelevant to ethical social work practice.
questions.explanation: Before using facial recognition, secure explicit informed consent from those whose faces will be analyzed. Explain the technology honestly, including known limitations and how data will be protected and used.

nodes.title: Course Complete: Computer Vision as a Social Work Tool
nodes.content: Congratulations on completing this course on computer vision fundamentals in social work. You now understand how classification, detection, and recognition technologies work, their potential applications in practice, and—most importantly—the ethical considerations you must navigate. Remember: technology is powerful, but your professional judgment, commitment to client self-determination, informed consent, and attention to bias and equity are what make you an effective social worker. As computer vision tools become more prevalent, your critical understanding of both their capabilities and limitations will help you advocate for clients, make informed decisions, and use technology as a tool that truly serves your practice values.
